sourceMovie.title: Ted
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Ted for the same reason it broke through in 2012: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a fantasy rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Seth MacFarlane keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The comedy and fantasy blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Ted 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Ted fans keep landing on Ted 2 (2015) — the comedy is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Ruby Sparks
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Ruby Sparks draws from the same comedy well as Ted, channeling fantasy in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2012 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: The Science of Sleep
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Science of Sleep (2006) captures the comedy appeal of Ted without simply imitating it. The fantasy streak running through both films is what puts The Science of Sleep at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Arizona Dream
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The comedy appeal of Ted — that funny edge, the fantasy — translates cleanly into Arizona Dream (1993). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: A Midsummer Night's Dream
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind A Midsummer Night's Dream (1999) clearly understood what makes comedy work — the same quality that keeps Ted rewatchable. Expect the same funny rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Back to the Future
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Back to the Future (1985) is the kind of film Ted fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The comedy overlap is real, but it's the funny emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Princess Mononoke
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Princess Mononoke shares Ted's gift for fantasy — the 1997 film delivers adventure without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Love, Rosie
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Ted compelling — the comedy, the romance — shows up in Love, Rosie (2014) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that funny storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Ghost
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The fantasy energy of Ted is alive in Ghost (1990), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a tense payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Billy Elliot
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Billy Elliot (2000) operates in the same comedy as Ted, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The funny payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

faq.question: What should I watch after Ted?
faq.answer: Try Ted 2, Ruby Sparks, The Science of Sleep—each shares DNA with Ted while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Ted?
faq.answer: Ted (2012) is best described as Comedy, Fantasy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Ted part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Ted Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
